claim: Numeric ratings (1-10 scales) perform poorly for LLM-as-a-judge evaluations compared to small sets of word labels (yes/no, positive/negative/neutral)
actionable: Use categorical labels with small sets of word choices instead of numeric scales when designing LLM judge evaluation criteria

claim: Do not use the same model for both your application and your judge—it's like letting a student grade their own essay
actionable: If your app uses GPT-4 mini, select a different provider like Claude for your judge model to avoid bias

claim: Reasoning models like OpenAI o3 or DeepSeek R1 are often overkill for LLM-as-a-judge evaluation tasks compared to traditional large models
actionable: Start with traditional large language models (GPT-4, Claude 3.5) for judge models; only upgrade to reasoning models if standard models show insufficient alignment

claim: Limit context window to 4,000 tokens or less for LLM judge prompts; models struggle with reasoning over longer contexts even with million-token windows
actionable: Trim your judge prompt and context documents to stay under 4K tokens; avoid passing entire knowledge bases into the judge

claim: Catching non-deterministic LLM judge responses by running evaluations 10+ times on small subsets reveals which examples are ambiguous and need clearer evaluation criteria
actionable: When setting up evaluations, run your judge 10-50 times on a subset and analyze variability to identify examples needing clearer criteria in your prompts

claim: Broader catch-all metrics like 'quality' fail because LLMs don't know what to focus on and results aren't actionable for debugging specific application failures
actionable: Split broad metrics into specific, measurable criteria (e.g., 'faithfulness to source' rather than 'quality'); ensure each metric points to a fixable part of your system

claim: Having 10+ overlapping or correlated metrics (e.g., fluency, style, tone, politeness) causes cognitive overload and wastes tokens without adding signal
actionable: Limit yourself to a handful of focused metrics tied to real business-critical failure modes; remove metrics that overlap or measure model strengths unlikely to fail

claim: Writing evaluation prompts as guidelines for a human annotator (with role, context, readable structure) is more effective than technical instruction sets
actionable: Structure your judge prompt like you're instructing a human annotator: give it a role, provide context about your application's goals, keep it organized and human-readable

claim: Human-annotation golden datasets are essential for meta-evaluation; comparing LLM judge labels to human labels is the only reliable way to build trust in evals
actionable: Invest time in manually annotating a golden dataset on your evaluation criteria; use it to calculate judge alignment and identify systematic failure modes

claim: Structured outputs (JSON schemas) eliminate the need for prompt-based format instructions and reduce parsing errors compared to regex matching
actionable: Use structured output/tool calling to enforce response format; avoid wasting prompt tokens on format instructions that may confuse the model

claim: Imperfect evals that are partial, incomplete, and noisy are better than no eval, and there is always room for improvement through iteration
actionable: Start with rough evaluations on a few key metrics; don't wait for perfect criteria before measuring—iterate based on results
